examples.explanation: Initial: [5, 10, -5]. Pass 1: i=0: 5>0, 10>0 → no collision. i=1: 10>0, -5<0 → collision. |10| vs |-5|: 10 > 5, so -5 destroyed. Remove -5 → [5, 10]. Pass 2: i=0: 5>0, 10>0 → no collision. No collisions in full pass → done. Output: [5, 10].
Initial: [8, -8]. Pass 1: i=0: 8>0, -8<0 → collision. |8| vs |-8|: equal, both destroyed. Remove both → []. Pass 2: array empty, no collisions → done. Output: [].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the asteroids repeatedly, simulating collisions by comparing adjacent pairs and removing destroyed asteroids until no collisions occur. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) if modifying input in-place, else O(n)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Asteroid Collision Simulation
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of asteroid collision simulation using repeated adjacent comparisons.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the asteroid collision problem. The intuition is to repeatedly scan through the asteroid array, comparing adjacent asteroids that might collide (positive moving right, negative moving left). When a collision occurs, we compare their absolute sizes: the larger survives, or both are destroyed if equal. We remove destroyed asteroids from the array and repeat the process until no collisions occur in a full pass. This approach works because collisions only happen between adjacent asteroids moving toward each other, and repeatedly checking ensures all possible collisions are resolved. The algorithm: 1. Initialize a flag 'collision_occurred' to True. 2. While collision_occurred is True: a. Set collision_occurred to False. b. For i from 0 to n-2: i. If asteroids[i] > 0 and asteroids[i+1] < 0 (collision condition): - Compare |asteroids[i]| and |asteroids[i+1]|. - If left larger: remove right asteroid. - If right larger: remove left asteroid. - If equal: remove both. - Set collision_occurred to True. - Adjust indices after removal. 3. Return the modified array. Time complexity is O(n²) in worst case (e.g., alternating positive/negative asteroids). Space complexity is O(1) if modifying in-place, O(n) if using a new array. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete collision resolution loop. Common mistakes include: not handling index adjustments properly after removals, infinite loops if collision detection is flawed, and missing edge cases like all positive or all negative asteroids. Real code pattern: while True: changed = False; for i in range(len(asteroids)-1): if asteroids[i] > 0 and asteroids[i+1] < 0: ... changed = True; if not changed: break.

examples.trace: Initial: [5, 10, -5]. Pass 1: i=0: 5>0, 10>0 → no collision. i=1: 10>0, -5<0 → collision. |10| vs |-5|: 10 > 5, so -5 destroyed. Remove -5 → [5, 10]. Pass 2: i=0: 5>0, 10>0 → no collision. No collisions in full pass → done. Output: [5, 10].
Initial: [8, -8]. Pass 1: i=0: 8>0, -8<0 → collision. |8| vs |-8|: equal, both destroyed. Remove both → []. Pass 2: array empty, no collisions → done. Output: [].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Adjacent Collision Detection and Resolution
ladders.desc: Detect and resolve collisions between two adjacent asteroids.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine handles the core collision logic between two adjacent asteroids. The intuition is that collisions only occur when a positive asteroid (moving right) is immediately followed by a negative asteroid (moving left), as they move toward each other. For any other pair (both positive, both negative, or negative followed by positive), no collision happens. When a collision is detected, we compare the absolute sizes: the larger asteroid survives, or both are destroyed if equal. This works because in a collision, the asteroid with greater mass dominates. The algorithm: 1. Check if asteroid at index i is positive and asteroid at index i+1 is negative. 2. If yes: a. Compare |asteroids[i]| and |asteroids[i+1]|. b. If left larger: remove right asteroid. c. If right larger: remove left asteroid. d. If equal: remove both. 3. Return the modified array and a boolean indicating if a collision occurred. Time complexity is O(1) per check, but removal may be O(n) if shifting elements. Space complexity is O(1) for the comparison. This connects to L0 by being the inner operation in the brute force loop. Common mistakes include: forgetting to check the direction condition (only positive→negative collides), incorrect absolute value comparisons, and not handling equal sizes correctly. Real code pattern: def resolve_collision(asteroids, i): if asteroids[i] > 0 and asteroids[i+1] < 0: left_size = abs(asteroids[i]); right_size = abs(asteroids[i+1]); if left_size > right_size: remove asteroids[i+1]; elif right_size > left_size: remove asteroids[i]; else: remove both; return True, asteroids; return False, asteroids.

examples.trace: Initial: [5, -3]. Check i=0: 5>0 and -3<0 → collision. |5| vs |-3|: 5 > 3, so -3 destroyed. Remove -3 → [5]. Output: [5].
Initial: [2, -5]. Check i=0: 2>0 and -5<0 → collision. |2| vs |-5|: 2 < 5, so 2 destroyed. Remove 2 → [-5]. Output: [-5].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Adjacent Pair Comparison
ladders.desc: Repeatedly scan through array to find and resolve all collisions.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the iterative process of scanning the asteroid array to handle collisions. The intuition is that after resolving one collision, new adjacent pairs may form that could collide, so we need to repeat the scan until no collisions are found in a complete pass. This ensures all possible collisions are resolved, as collisions can cascade (e.g., [5, -3, 2] → after removing -3, 5 and 2 become adjacent but don't collide). The algorithm: 1. Set a flag 'changed' to True. 2. While changed is True: a. Set changed to False. b. For i from 0 to n-2: i. Check if asteroids[i] and asteroids[i+1] collide (positive followed by negative). ii. If yes, resolve the collision (remove one or both asteroids). iii. Set changed to True. iv. Adjust loop index if needed after removal. 3. The loop ends when a full pass finds no collisions. Time complexity is O(n²) in worst case because each pass is O(n) and we may need O(n) passes. Space complexity is O(1) if modifying in-place. This connects to L1 by repeatedly applying the collision resolution sub-routine. Common mistakes include: not resetting the changed flag properly, infinite loops if collision detection is incorrect, and off-by-one errors in the loop bounds. Real code pattern: changed = True; while changed: changed = False; i = 0; while i < len(asteroids)-1: if asteroids[i] > 0 and asteroids[i+1] < 0: ... changed = True; i += 1.

examples.trace: Initial: [5, -3, 2]. Pass 1: i=0: 5>0, -3<0 → collision. |5|>|-3|, remove -3 → [5, 2]. changed=True. i=1: (now array [5,2]) 5>0, 2>0 → no collision. End pass. Pass 2: i=0: 5>0, 2>0 → no collision. changed=False → done. Output: [5, 2].

Initial: [1, -2, 3, -4]. Pass 1: i=0: 1>0, -2<0 → collision. |1|<|-2|, remove 1 → [-2, 3, -4]. changed=True. i=1: -2<0, 3>0 → no collision. i=2: 3>0, -4<0 → collision. |3|<|-4|, remove 3 → [-2, -4]. changed=True. Pass 2: i=0: -2<0, -4<0 → no collision. changed=False → done. Output: [-2, -4].

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Modification with Element Removal
ladders.desc: Remove elements from an array while iterating, handling index adjustments.
ladders.explanation: This level teaches how to modify an array by removing elements during iteration, which is crucial for the brute force approach. The intuition is that when we remove an element, all subsequent elements shift left, so we must adjust our loop index to avoid skipping elements or going out of bounds. This is a common pattern in array manipulation problems. The algorithm: 1. Iterate through the array with an index i. 2. When an element at position i needs to be removed: a. Use array removal operation (e.g., pop(i) in Python). b. Decrement i by 1 if needed to re-check the new element at position i. 3. When an element at position i+1 needs to be removed: a. Remove it (e.g., pop(i+1)). b. No change to i typically, as the next element moves into position i+1. Time complexity for removal is O(n) in worst case due to shifting elements. Space complexity is O(1) for in-place modification. This connects to L2 by enabling the collision resolution to update the array. Common mistakes include: not adjusting i after removal, causing infinite loops or missed elements; off-by-one errors; and using inefficient removal methods. Real code pattern: i = 0; while i < len(arr): if condition_to_remove(arr[i]): arr.pop(i); else: i += 1.

examples.trace: Initial: [1, -2, 3, -4, 5], i=0. i=0: 1 not negative → i=1. i=1: -2 negative → remove at i=1 → [1, 3, -4, 5], i stays 1. i=1: 3 not negative → i=2. i=2: -4 negative → remove at i=2 → [1, 3, 5], i stays 2. i=2: 5 not negative → i=3. i=3 >= len → done. Output: [1, 3, 5].

Initial: [0, 1, 0, 2, 0], i=0. i=0: 0 equals 0 → remove at i=0 → [1, 0, 2, 0], i stays 0. i=0: 1 not 0 → i=1. i=1: 0 equals 0 → remove at i=1 → [1, 2, 0], i stays 1. i=1: 2 not 0 → i=2. i=2: 0 equals 0 → remove at i=2 → [1, 2], i stays 2. i=2 >= len → done. Output: [1, 2].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Conditionally Remove Array Elements
ladders.desc: Compare two adjacent array elements and remove one based on a condition.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental operation of comparing two adjacent elements in an array and conditionally removing one. The intuition is that in many array problems, decisions are made based on comparisons between neighbors, and removal is a common action. Here, we specifically look at the case relevant to asteroid collisions: if the left element is positive and the right is negative, we compare their absolute values and remove the smaller (or both if equal). This operation is a building block for the collision resolution. The algorithm: 1. Check if arr[i] > 0 and arr[i+1] < 0. 2. If yes: a. Compute left_size = |arr[i]|, right_size = |arr[i+1]|. b. If left_size > right_size: remove arr[i+1]. c. If right_size > left_size: remove arr[i]. d. If equal: remove both arr[i] and arr[i+1]. 3. Return the modified array. Time complexity is O(1) for the comparison, but removal may be O(n). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 by being a specific case of array modification. Common mistakes include: using the original values instead of absolute values for comparison, incorrect removal indices, and not handling the equal case. Real code pattern: if arr[i] > 0 and arr[i+1] < 0: if abs(arr[i]) > abs(arr[i+1]): arr.pop(i+1); elif abs(arr[i]) < abs(arr[i+1]): arr.pop(i); else: arr.pop(i+1); arr.pop(i).

examples.input: Array: [5, -3], compare and remove at i=0
examples.trace: Initial: [5, -3]. Check: 5>0 and -3<0 → true. |5|=5, |-3|=3. 5 > 3, so remove right element at index 1 → [5]. Output: [5].

examples.input: Array: [2, -5], compare and remove at i=0
examples.trace: Initial: [2, -5]. Check: 2>0 and -5<0 → true. |2|=2, |-5|=5. 2 < 5, so remove left element at index 0 → [-5]. Output: [-5].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Iteration and Element Comparison
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array and compare adjacent elements.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for the brute force approach: iterating through an array and comparing each element with its neighbor. The intuition is that many array problems involve examining relationships between adjacent elements, such as finding pairs that satisfy a condition. Here, we focus on the simple act of looping through the array and accessing consecutive elements. This is a prerequisite for detecting asteroid collisions. The algorithm: 1. Initialize a loop index i from 0 to n-2 (where n is array length). 2. For each i, access elements at positions i and i+1. 3. Perform a comparison (e.g., check if they are moving toward each other). 4. No modifications are made at this level—just observation. Time complexity is O(n) for a single pass. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L4 by providing the iteration framework for the comparison and removal operation. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors (iterating beyond n-1), not handling empty or single-element arrays, and incorrect indexing. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)-1): left = arr[i]; right = arr[i+1]; if condition(left, right): ...

examples.input: Array: [1, 2, 3, 4], check if each pair is increasing
examples.trace: Initial: [1, 2, 3, 4]. i=0: compare 1 and 2 → 1<2, increasing. i=1: compare 2 and 3 → 2<3, increasing. i=2: compare 3 and 4 → 3<4, increasing. All pairs are increasing.

examples.input: Array: [5, -3, 0], check if left positive and right negative
examples.trace: Initial: [5, -3, 0]. i=0: left=5>0, right=-3<0 → condition true. i=1: left=-3<0, right=0 not negative → condition false. Only the first pair satisfies.

testCases.input: Array: [1, -1, 1], check if left positive and right negative

approaches.name: Stack Simulation O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a stack to process asteroids in order: push positive asteroids, and for negative asteroids, pop and compare with positive ones on the stack to handle collisions. This efficiently resolves collisions in a single pass.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Asteroid Collision Simulation with Stack
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of asteroid collision simulation using stack approach
ladders.explanation: The asteroid collision problem requires simulating collisions between asteroids moving in a line, where positive values move right, negative values move left, and collisions occur when they meet. The stack simulation approach processes asteroids in order, using a stack to efficiently handle collisions in O(n) time. The intuition is that we only need to compare each asteroid with previous ones that could collide with it - specifically, when we encounter a negative asteroid moving left, it can only collide with positive asteroids to its left that haven't been destroyed yet. The stack maintains these potential collision candidates. For each asteroid: if it's positive, we push it onto the stack since it won't collide with anything to its right yet. If it's negative, we compare it with positive asteroids on the stack (which are to its left). We pop and compare until either: the negative asteroid is destroyed by a larger positive one, it destroys all positive asteroids on the stack, or it survives and we push it. The key insight is that negative asteroids only need to check the stack, not the entire array, because any asteroid that would have been to its right has already been processed and either destroyed or moved past. The algorithm processes each asteroid exactly once, with each asteroid being pushed and popped at most once, giving O(n) time complexity. The space complexity is O(n) in the worst case when no collisions occur. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle the case where asteroids have equal magnitude (both destroyed), incorrectly handling the order of comparisons, or not properly managing the stack when multiple collisions occur in sequence. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the collision resolution subroutine with the overall stack management.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Process 5 (positive) → stack = [5]
Step 2: Process 10 (positive) → stack = [5, 10]
Step 3: Process -5 (negative) → Compare with top 10: |-5| < 10 → -5 destroyed, stack remains [5, 10]
Final: stack = [5, 10]

Step 1: Process 8 (positive) → stack = [8]
Step 2: Process -8 (negative) → Compare with top 8: |-8| = 8 → both destroyed, pop 8, stack = []
Final: stack = []

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Resolve Collision Between Current Asteroid and Stack
ladders.desc: Implement the collision resolution logic between a negative asteroid and positive asteroids on stack
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine handles the core collision logic when encountering a negative asteroid. The intuition is that a negative asteroid (moving left) can only collide with positive asteroids (moving right) that are to its left and still exist. The stack contains these potential collision candidates in order from left to right. The algorithm: while stack is not empty AND top of stack is positive AND current negative asteroid's magnitude is greater than top's magnitude, pop the top (destroy the positive asteroid). After this loop, if stack is empty or top is negative, push current asteroid (it survived all collisions). If top is positive and has equal magnitude, pop it (both destroyed). If top is positive and has greater magnitude, do nothing (current asteroid destroyed). The key insight is processing in this order ensures we handle all possible collisions correctly. Time complexity is O(k) where k is number of popped elements, but amortized O(1) per asteroid. Space complexity is O(1) extra space. Common mistakes: not checking stack emptiness before accessing top, incorrect comparison order, or missing the equal magnitude case. This feeds into L0 by being the critical component called for each negative asteroid.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check stack not empty (true) and top is positive (10) and |-5| < 10 → current destroyed, stop
Step 2: No push, stack remains [5, 10]

Step 1: Check stack not empty (true) and top positive (8) and |-10| > 8 → pop 8, stack = [3]
Step 2: Check stack not empty (true) and top positive (3) and |-10| > 3 → pop 3, stack = []
Step 3: Stack empty → push -10, stack = [-10]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Stack Maintains Surviving Asteroids in Collision Order
ladders.desc: Understand how stack preserves correct order of surviving asteroids through collisions
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that a stack naturally maintains the correct relative order of asteroids that survive collisions. The intuition: when processing asteroids left to right, the stack always contains asteroids that haven't been destroyed and are in the correct final order. Positive asteroids are pushed immediately since they move right and won't collide with future asteroids until a negative one appears. When a negative asteroid appears, it compares with the stack (which contains positive asteroids to its left). The stack's LIFO property ensures we compare with the closest positive asteroid first, which is correct because collisions happen between adjacent asteroids. After processing all collisions, the stack contains exactly the surviving asteroids in their original relative order. Why does this work? Because any asteroid that would have been destroyed in a collision is removed from the stack, and asteroids that don't collide remain in order. The algorithm: initialize empty stack, for each asteroid: if positive, push; if negative, resolve collisions using the L1 subroutine. Time O(n), space O(n). Common mistakes: trying to use a queue instead of stack (wouldn't give correct collision order), or not understanding why we only compare negative with stack. This is the central technique that makes L1's subroutine effective.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Process 3 → stack = [3]
Step 2: Process 5 → stack = [3, 5]
Step 3: Process -2 → compare with 5: |-2| < 5 → -2 destroyed, stack = [3, 5]
Final order preserved: [3, 5]

Step 1: Process 4 → stack = [4]
Step 2: Process -3 → compare with 4: |-3| < 4 → -3 destroyed, stack = [4]
Step 3: Process 2 → positive, push → stack = [4, 2]
Final order: [4, 2]

ladders.title: Building Block — Processing Array with Conditional Stack Operations
ladders.desc: Implement array traversal with stack pushes/pops based on conditions
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing an array while performing different stack operations based on conditions. The intuition is that we need to process each element sequentially and decide whether to push it, pop from stack, or do both based on specific rules. The algorithm: initialize empty stack, iterate through array, for each element: check if it's positive → push to stack; if negative → enter a while loop that pops from stack while certain conditions hold (stack not empty, top positive, and magnitude comparisons). This pattern of conditional stack manipulation is fundamental to many stack-based algorithms. The key is understanding when to use a while loop vs if statement, and how the conditions determine the operations. Time O(n), space O(n). Common mistakes: infinite loops from incorrect termination conditions, off-by-one errors in comparisons, or not handling edge cases like empty stack. This builds toward L2 by providing the framework for the collision logic.

examples.input: [2, -1, 3] with rule: push positives, for negatives pop while top > 0 and |current| > top
examples.trace:
Step 1: 2 positive → push, stack = [2]
Step 2: -1 negative → while stack not empty and top (2) > 0 and |-1| > 2? false → push -1, stack = [2, -1]
Step 3: 3 positive → push, stack = [2, -1, 3]
Final: [2, -1, 3]

Step 1: 4 positive → push, stack = [4]
Step 2: -5 negative → while stack not empty and top (4) > 0 and |-5| > 4? true → pop 4, stack = []
→ stack empty, push -5, stack = [-5]
Step 3: 2 positive → push, stack = [-5, 2]
Final: [-5, 2]

testCases.input: [5, -3] push pos, for neg pop while top>0 and |curr|>top

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push/Pop with Magnitude Comparison
ladders.desc: Implement fundamental stack operations with numerical comparisons
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves basic stack manipulation (push, pop, peek) combined with comparing absolute values of numbers. The intuition is that we need to not only manage stack elements but also compare their magnitudes to make decisions. The algorithm: maintain a stack of integers. Operations include: push(value) adds to top, pop() removes top, peek() returns top without removing, and isEmpty() checks if empty. The key addition is comparing absolute values: |a| > |b|, |a| == |b|, |a| < |b|. This is crucial because asteroid collisions depend on size comparisons. Implementation involves careful handling of positive vs negative signs when comparing magnitudes. Time O(1) per operation, space O(n). Common mistakes: confusing value with magnitude, incorrect comparison operators, or not handling stack boundaries. This supports L3 by providing the primitive operations needed for conditional processing.

examples.input: Push 5, push 3, compare |top| with |-4|, pop if |top| < |-4|
examples.trace:
Step 1: Push 5 → stack = [5]
Step 2: Push 3 → stack = [5, 3]
Step 3: Compare |3| < |-4|? 3 < 4 → true → pop 3 → stack = [5]
Final: [5]

examples.input: Push -2, push 4, compare |top| with | -1|, pop if |top| < | -1|
examples.trace:
Step 1: Push -2 → stack = [-2]
Step 2: Push 4 → stack = [-2, 4]
Step 3: Compare |4| < |-1|? 4 < 1 → false → no pop
Final: [-2, 4]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Stack Data Structure and Absolute Value
ladders.desc: Understand basic stack operations and absolute value concept
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers two prerequisite concepts: stack data structure and absolute value of numbers. A stack is a LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) data structure with three basic operations: push (add to top), pop (remove from top), and peek (view top without removing). It can be implemented using arrays or linked lists. The intuition is like a stack of plates - you add and remove from the top. Absolute value, denoted |x|, is the non-negative value of x without regard to sign. For example, |5| = 5, |-5| = 5. This is crucial for comparing asteroid sizes regardless of direction. Beginners should practice: creating a stack, pushing numbers, popping them, and comparing absolute values. Time complexity: O(1) for all basic operations. Space complexity: O(n) to store n elements. Common mistakes: trying to access empty stack, confusing stack with queue (FIFO), or miscalculating absolute values of negative numbers. This is the building block for L4's operations.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Push 3 → stack = [3]
Step 2: Push -2 → stack = [3, -2]
Step 3: Pop removes -2 → stack = [3]
Step 4: Peek returns 3

Step 1: | -7| = 7
Step 2: |4| = 4
Step 3: Compare 7 > 4

approaches.name: Two Pointer In-Place O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers to simulate collisions directly in the input array: one pointer tracks the current position, and another manages the result, overwriting elements as collisions are resolved. This reduces space usage while maintaining linear time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Asteroid Collision with Two Pointer In-Place O(n)
ladders.desc: Complete solution using two pointers to simulate asteroid collisions directly in the input array.
ladders.explanation: This problem simulates asteroid collisions where positive values move right, negative values move left, and collisions occur when they meet. The two-pointer in-place approach uses a result pointer (res) to track the last stable asteroid in the modified array and a current pointer (i) to iterate through asteroids. When a collision occurs (res asteroid positive, current asteroid negative), we compare absolute values: if current is larger, we overwrite the res asteroid and check previous collisions; if res is larger, we skip current; if equal, we remove both by decrementing res. This approach modifies the array in-place, using O(1) extra space while maintaining O(n) time complexity. The intuition is that we only need to track potential collisions with the last stable asteroid, similar to stack logic but without extra storage. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize res = -1 (no stable asteroids yet). 2. For each asteroid from i=0 to n-1: a. If res == -1 or no collision (res asteroid negative or current asteroid positive), increment res and copy current asteroid. b. Else (collision: res positive, current negative): Compare absolute values. If current > res: overwrite res asteroid with current, decrement res to check previous collisions (loop). If res > current: skip current (do nothing). If equal: decrement res (both destroyed). 3. Return array slice [0:res+1]. Time: O(n) as each asteroid processed at most twice. Space: O(1) excluding input. Connection to L1: This integrates the collision resolution subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling the loop to check previous collisions after overwriting, or incorrect collision detection. Real code pattern: def asteroidCollision(asteroids): res = -1; for i in range(len(asteroids)): while res >= 0 and asteroids[res] > 0 and asteroids[i] < 0: if abs(asteroids[i]) > asteroids[res]: res -= 1; continue; elif abs(asteroids[i]) < asteroids[res]: break; else: res -= 1; break; else: res += 1; asteroids[res] = asteroids[i]; return asteroids[:res+1].

examples.trace: Start: asteroids = [5, 10, -5], res = -1. i=0: res=-1, so res=0, asteroids[0]=5. Array: [5, 10, -5]. i=1: res=0, asteroids[0]=5>0, asteroids[1]=10>0 (no collision), res=1, asteroids[1]=10. Array: [5, 10, -5]. i=2: res=1, asteroids[1]=10>0, asteroids[2]=-5<0 (collision). Compare: abs(-5)=5 < 10, so skip current (do nothing). End: res=1, return [5,10].

Start: asteroids = [8, -8], res = -1. i=0: res=-1, so res=0, asteroids[0]=8. Array: [8, -8]. i=1: res=0, asteroids[0]=8>0, asteroids[1]=-8<0 (collision). Compare: abs(-8)=8 == 8, so decrement res: res=-1 (both destroyed). End: res=-1, return [].

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Collision Resolution with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Resolve collision between last stable asteroid and current asteroid using two pointers.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine handles the core collision logic when the last stable asteroid (at index res) is positive and the current asteroid (at index i) is negative. It compares their absolute values to determine the outcome: if current is larger, it overwrites the res asteroid and recursively checks previous collisions; if res is larger, it discards current; if equal, it removes both. The intuition is that collisions only affect the most recent stable asteroid that could interact, mimicking a stack's LIFO behavior but in-place. Step-by-step: 1. Check if collision exists: res >= 0, asteroids[res] > 0, asteroids[i] < 0. 2. If collision: a. Compare abs(asteroids[i]) and asteroids[res]. b. If current larger: set asteroids[res] = asteroids[i], decrement res to check previous collisions (loop). c. If res larger: do nothing (skip current). d. If equal: decrement res (both destroyed). 3. If no collision or after resolution: increment res and copy current asteroid. Time: O(k) where k is number of collisions checked, amortized O(1) per asteroid. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each asteroid in the full solution. Common mistakes: Forgetting to loop after overwriting, or mishandling equal case. Real code pattern: while res >= 0 and asteroids[res] > 0 and asteroids[i] < 0: if abs(asteroids[i]) > asteroids[res]: res -= 1; continue; elif abs(asteroids[i]) < asteroids[res]: break; else: res -= 1; break; else: res += 1; asteroids[res] = asteroids[i].

examples.trace: Start: res=0, asteroids=[10, -5], i=1. Check collision: asteroids[0]=10>0, asteroids[1]=-5<0, so collision. Compare: abs(-5)=5 < 10, so res larger, break loop. No else executed (since collision handled). End: res=0, asteroids unchanged.

Start: res=1, asteroids=[5, 10, -15], i=2. Check collision: asteroids[1]=10>0, asteroids[2]=-15<0, so collision. Compare: abs(-15)=15 > 10, so current larger. Set asteroids[1]=-15, res=0. Loop: res=0, asteroids[0]=5>0, asteroids[2]=-15<0 (still i=2), collision. Compare: abs(-15)=15 > 5, so current larger. Set asteroids[0]=-15, res=-1. Loop ends as res=-1. End: res=-1, asteroids=[-15, -15, -15] (only first two matter).

ladders.title: Core Logic — In-Place Array Modification with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Use two pointers to overwrite array elements while resolving collisions.
ladders.explanation: This technique uses a result pointer (res) to track the index of the last stable asteroid in the array, and a current pointer (i) to iterate through asteroids. Instead of using a separate stack, we modify the input array in-place: when an asteroid is stable, we copy it to position res+1 and increment res; when a collision destroys asteroids, we decrement res to remove them. The intuition is that the array prefix up to res represents the stable state, and we can reuse space for new elements. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize res = -1. 2. For each i from 0 to n-1: a. Determine if asteroid at i is stable (no collision with asteroid at res). b. If stable: res += 1; asteroids[res] = asteroids[i]. c. If not stable: handle collision (see L1), which may update res. 3. Final result is asteroids[0:res+1]. Time: O(n) as each asteroid processed once, but collision handling may cause res to move backward. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This provides the framework where collision resolution is applied. Common mistakes: Incorrectly updating res without copying, or off-by-one errors in array bounds. Real code pattern: res = -1; for i in range(n): # collision logic here; if stable: res += 1; arr[res] = arr[i].

examples.trace: Start: arr=[2, -1, 3], res=-1. i=0: stable (res=-1), res=0, arr[0]=2. Array: [2, -1, 3]. i=1: res=0, arr[0]=2>0, arr[1]=-1<0 (collision). Compare: abs(-1)=1 < 2, so skip. res unchanged. i=2: res=0, arr[0]=2>0, arr[2]=3>0 (no collision), stable. res=1, arr[1]=3. Array: [2, 3, 3]. End: res=1, return [2,3].

Start: arr=[-3, 2, 1], res=-1. i=0: stable (res=-1), res=0, arr[0]=-3. Array: [-3, 2, 1]. i=1: res=0, arr[0]=-3<0, arr[1]=2>0 (no collision), stable. res=1, arr[1]=2. Array: [-3, 2, 1]. i=2: res=1, arr[1]=2>0, arr[2]=1>0 (no collision), stable. res=2, arr[2]=1. Array: [-3, 2, 1]. End: res=2, return [-3,2,1].

ladders.title: Building Block — Collision Detection for Asteroids
ladders.desc: Determine if two asteroids will collide based on their directions and positions.
ladders.explanation: This concept checks whether two adjacent asteroids in the simulation will collide. Asteroids are represented by integers: positive values move to the right, negative values move to the left. A collision occurs only when a right-moving asteroid (positive) is to the left of a left-moving asteroid (negative). In array terms, for indices i and j where i < j, a collision happens if asteroid[i] > 0 and asteroid[j] < 0. The intuition is that opposite directions cause them to meet, while same directions or negative-then-positive pairs never collide. Step-by-step: Given two asteroids a and b: 1. If a > 0 and b < 0: collision possible (they move towards each other). 2. Else: no collision (both move right, both move left, or left then right). Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This detection is used in the in-place logic to decide when to apply collision resolution. Common mistakes: Confusing directions or missing that collision requires specific order. Real code pattern: def willCollide(a, b): return a > 0 and b < 0.

examples.trace: a=3 (positive, moves right), b=-2 (negative, moves left). Since a is to the left of b in simulation, they move towards each other: collision occurs.
a=-4 (negative, moves left), b=1 (positive, moves right). a moves left away from b, b moves right away from a: no collision.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Absolute Values of Integers
ladders.desc: Compare the magnitudes of two integers, ignoring their signs.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves taking the absolute value of integers (making them non-negative) and comparing which is larger. For integers a and b, compute abs(a) and abs(b), then compare: if abs(a) > abs(b), a has larger magnitude; if abs(a) < abs(b), b has larger magnitude; if equal, magnitudes are same. The intuition is that in asteroid collisions, magnitude determines the outcome regardless of direction. Step-by-step: 1. Compute abs_a = abs(a), abs_b = abs(b). 2. Compare abs_a and abs_b using >, <, or ==. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison is used in collision detection to decide which asteroid survives. Common mistakes: Forgetting to take absolute values or confusing with signed comparison. Real code pattern: if abs(a) > abs(b): # a larger; elif abs(a) < abs(b): # b larger; else: # equal.

examples.trace: abs(5)=5, abs(-3)=3. Compare: 5 > 3, so a has larger magnitude.
abs(-4)=4, abs(4)=4. Compare: 4 == 4, so magnitudes equal.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Positive and Negative Integers as Directions
ladders.desc: Understand how positive and negative integers represent movement directions.
ladders.explanation: In this problem, integers represent asteroids: positive integers (greater than 0) mean the asteroid is moving to the right, and negative integers (less than 0) mean it is moving to the left. Zero can be considered stationary or neutral. The intuition is that sign indicates direction, while magnitude indicates size. For example, +3 is a size-3 asteroid moving right, -5 is size-5 moving left. This is a simple mapping: if number > 0, direction = right; if number < 0, direction = left. Step-by-step: Given an integer x: 1. If x > 0: direction is right. 2. If x < 0: direction is left. 3. If x == 0: direction is neutral (no movement). Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This direction concept is used to determine when collisions occur. Common mistakes: Confusing sign with value or misinterpreting zero. Real code pattern: if x > 0: print('right'); elif x < 0: print('left'); else: print('neutral').

examples.trace: 4 > 0, so positive integer: direction is right.
-2 < 0, so negative integer: direction is left.
